title: sports-registry — retention windows
description: Two shapes of retention. Platform-seeded rows are PERMANENT — they never hard-delete (only deactivate). Tenant custom rows follow the standard soft-delete → archived → hard-delete lifecycle. Audit rows survive their source row for compliance replay: 2 years for regular mutations, 7 years for SportPrefixResolutionFailed events (dangling-reference signal).
compliance_regimes_referenced: SOC 2 CC7.2 (system monitoring — reconciler audit trail)
gdpr Art. 5(1)(c) (data minimisation — the registry stores taxonomy only, not user data)

cold.state: deactivated (is_active=false); hidden from tenant picker; still resolves via SportKeyResolver for historical references

purge.note: Platform-seeded rows are the taxonomy contract. Even deactivated + zero downstream references, they survive indefinitely. Rationale: platform history is an operational property — someone somewhere may still be reading an old audit log or historical enrollment that references a deactivated slug. Hard-deleting would introduce ambiguity where a resolver call returns SPORT_KEY_UNRESOLVED for a slug that legitimately existed at write time.

retention.entity: Sport (tenant custom, is_platform_seeded=false, tenant_id NOT NULL)

hot.days: while deleted_at IS NULL AND is_active=true

cold.state: is_active=false OR deleted_at IS NOT NULL; hidden from tenant picker; still resolves via SportKeyResolver for historical references

purge.trigger: is_active=false AND updated_at + custom_deactivated_days past AND no downstream reference remains
purge.note: If any downstream row (Team, AthleteEnrollment, etc. listed in the reconciler config) still references this Sport's slug, the job SKIPS the row + logs the pending purge. Explicit `sports:registry:deactivate --force-hard-delete` bypasses the retention window (support tooling; requires ops sign-off) but never applies to platform-seeded rows.

hot.days: while deleted_at IS NULL AND is_active=true

cold.state: is_active=false OR deleted_at IS NOT NULL

hot.days: while deleted_at IS NULL AND is_active=true

cold.state: is_active=false OR deleted_at IS NOT NULL

purge.note: Elevated retention because these audit rows are the compliance-replay signal for downstream Wave 3 data integrity. A tenant asking 'why was athlete X's enrollment shown as unresolvable' should be able to reconstruct the sport_key state from these rows regardless of what happened to the Sport row itself.

purge.trigger: TTL expiry OR invalidation on Registry mutation

notes: Platform-seeded row permanence is deliberate. A tenant with a 5-year-old enrollment that references `sport_key='football'` should still resolve even if the platform later decided to split football into 'soccer' and 'american_football'. The old slug remains active (deactivated but resolvable) forever.

The 730-day (2-year) custom-deactivation retention is optimised for tenant custom safety — a customer who deactivated a custom sport by mistake has 2 years to restore it before the hard-delete window opens. Enterprise tenants may extend via a support-ticket config override.

PurgeArchivedRegistryRowsJob runs WEEKLY (not daily) because the workload is trivial — most tenants never deactivate customs, and customs never quite make the FK-clear + retention-past bar together. Weekly is enough.

Deleting an entire tenant cascades platform-seeded rows unchanged (they carry tenant_id NULL) + custom rows via FK CASCADE. The PreventCustomRegistryOrphansOnTenantErased hook writes a diagnostic before the cascade fires; nothing to prevent (custom rows can't be referenced from outside the tenant).
